title: How we'll spend the day
bullets: 9:00  —  Arrival, breakfast, introductions.
9:30  —  What to expect across the cohort.
10:00  —  Session 1 — Prompting & design.
11:45  —  Session 2 — Defining AI problems.
1:00  —  Lunch.
2:00  —  Session 3 — Prototyping your solution.
3:45  —  Session 4 — Refining your solution.
4:45  —  Show & tell — demo what you built today.

title: SEVEN organisations, One room
subtitle: We're going to spend a lot of time together over the next two months, let's start with some introductions!!

title: Let's start with introductions
card: Let's take 5 minutes to talk to each other across the table.
Your name and organisation.
Something that excites you, or worries you about AI.
question: Skepticism is welcome here. Naming a worry is as useful as naming a hope.

quote: A century ago, electricity went from a curiosity to the quiet power behind every industry. AI is on the same path — a general-purpose capability you plug into almost any kind of work.
attribution: "AI is the new electricity." — Andrew Ng

title: The Hype: A reality check
note: ugly-image — flag for reshoot (dated reaction-meme GIFs)

title: Where there’s hype, there’s a hustle
subtitle: “Become an AI millionaire — only ₹9.” Hype grows its own economy of shortcuts and gurus. Real skill isn’t for sale at a discount — it’s built by doing, which is exactly why you’re here.

title: Predictive AI vs. Generative AI
bullets: Predictive
What  learns from past data to forecast and classify.
Asks  “what’s likely?” — fraud, no-shows, a diagnosis.
In the wild  predicting how proteins fold to design new medicines.
Strongest with good data and a clear question.

title: Predictive AI vs. Generative AI
bullets: Generative
What  makes something new from a prompt — text, images, code.
Asks  “make me one” — a draft, a summary, a picture.
In the wild  Google’s “Nano Banana” turns a sentence into an image.
You steer; it produces the first draft.

quote: You know your work better than anyone in this room. Our job isn’t to tell you what to build, it’s to help you put AI to the work you already do.

title: What teams build in a programme like this
bullets: Build a policy  —  a usable internal AI policy for your org.
Automate the admin  —  reclaim hours from repetitive tasks.
Create & communicate  —  draft materials and content faster.
Answer & assist  —  handle repetitive questions at scale.
Learn & decide  —  turn your own data into insight.
kicker: We're a first cohort, so we're learning together but the destination is real, working tools you own and run.

title: The next two months
subtitle: From friction in your day-to-day to a tool you can run.

left.label: Focus
left.body: Explore where AI can help and build hands-on fluency before committing to a problem.

right.label: Goals
bullets: Explore high-impact AI use cases
Build fluency with prompting and core tools
Understand AI's strengths and limits
Spot workflows that feel unclear or manual

left.label: Focus
left.body: Narrow from many ideas to one clear, AI-solvable workflow.

right.label: Goals
bullets: Narrow to one workflow, your MVP
Break the work into steps
Define inputs, constraints, stakeholders
Describe what success looks like, and how you'll judge it

left.label: Focus
left.body: Turn the scoped workflow into a reusable AI prototype.

right.label: Goals
bullets: Build a reusable prototype aligned to the workflow
Apply structured prompting and context-setting
Test against real examples
Compare outputs to your success criteria and refine

left.label: Focus
left.body: Make it consistent, reliable, and ready for real use.

right.label: Goals
bullets: Strengthen consistency through testing
Find edge cases and failure modes
Add validation steps
Clarify how it fits your team's decisions and workflow

left.label: Focus
left.body: Show what you built and how it changes the work.

right.label: Goals
bullets: Present the workflow, prototype, and evidence of impact
Share how you measured success
Outline next steps for adoption and iteration

title: Where it all leads : DEMO DAY!!
subtitle: Four in-person sessions. One destination - 25th July 2026. A Celebration with your AI Solution Demos on how you’re enhancing impact and workflows. Built by your team, for your team.

title: Fluency takes time
bullets: Building fluency takes time  —  nobody walks in fluent; that’s the point of two months.
You learn best by doing  —  we build more than we lecture.
The fun comes in iterating  —  the second and third tries are where it gets good.

title: A quick word on ethics
subtitle: Guardrails first and then we experiment freely.

quote: We’re navigating a new frontier together. None of us has all the answers yet — and that’s exactly why we start by agreeing on how we’ll move.

title: You’re in control
subtitle: Adopt AI in the way that’s right for your organisation.

rows.body: free AI tools aren’t private by default. Beneficiary and donor data stays out unless the platform is secure and configured for it.

rows.lead: Keep a human in the loop
rows.body: until you fully understand how a tool behaves on your work, review outputs before relying on them.
out-of-the-box AI doesn’t reflect every context. Adapt it to truly serve the people you serve.

title: Prompting and design
subtitle: The shortest path from blank prompt to useful output.

title: Make your credits last
subtitle: Match the model to the task — and spend tokens like the budget they are.

left.label: Reach for the powerful one
bullets: Use it for hard reasoning, ambiguous problems, gnarly code, the tricky first draft.
Such as Claude Opus · ChatGPT's deep-reasoning mode.
Powerful models cost more per message — save them for when they earn it.

right.label: Default to the fast one
bullets: Use it for everyday drafting, summaries, reformatting, repetitive Q&A, quick edits.
Such as Claude Sonnet or Haiku · ChatGPT's fast mode.
Most of your day-to-day work never needs the expensive model.

title: Spend less, get more
bullets: Start a fresh chat for a new task — long histories re-send every earlier message, and you pay for them each time.
Be specific, not verbose — a clear, short prompt beats a long, rambling one.
Don't paste the whole document — give the one section the model actually needs.
Reuse your saved prompts — don't re-explain the same setup from scratch.
Turn off tools you're not using — web search and extras add tokens you may not need.

quote: Start with the smallest model that could do the job. Escalate only when it visibly struggles — most everyday work never needs the expensive one.
attribution: Right-sizing is the cheapest habit you'll build

title: Ask AI to solve a real work problem
card: Draft a grant application for the (fake) grant on the next slide.
The goal isn't perfection; it's experimentation.
Can you get AI to speak in your org's voice? Reflect your mission?
Show off if you know more than prompting already!
question: There is no single "right" prompt. Type anything. Revise as many times as you want.

title: The Bangalore Social Impact Grant
card: ₹1,00,000 · Open to all nonprofits
You have 10 minutes. Use AI to draft a response to the following:
Describe your organisation and its impact. (2,000 words or less)
How will you use this grant to increase your organisation's impact?
How will you measure the success of your impact?
question: The goal isn't a perfect application. It's this: Can you get AI to sound like your organisation? To reflect your mission, your community, your voice?

title: Reflect on that first attempt
card: What was your original prompt?
Did you revise or iterate on your prompt? (If so, how?)
What context did you give the AI about your organization?
If you did more than prompting, what did you do and why?
question: Reflect on that first attempt

title: Role, steps, output

rows.lead: Give it a role and a goal
rows.body: "You are a [add an expert, such as a "social media expert"] and your objective is to [add goal, such as "create LinkedIn posts about events"]"
"Here's the context of the situation: (context). To complete these instructions, follow these steps: Step 1, Step 2, Step 3 (etc)"
"When you are done, write your final response": A summary paragraph of the work done; 3 bullet points, one per next step

kicker: Try your prompt again with this new structure. Start a new conversation and ask it again using these ideas. You are refining your prompt - not trying to perfect the output.

title: What changed?
card: Role & Goal — where did you define the model's expertise or purpose?
Steps & Context — where did you guide how it works with you?
Output — where did you specify what "done" looks like?
question: Which part was hardest to make explicit, and why?

title: Welcome to the Prompt Cookbook from Decoded Futures!
bullets: Iterate on ideas with it
Have it ask you clarifying questions
Have it write the prompt for you

title: Break — 10 minutes
subtitle: Stretch, refill, compare notes. Back at the top of the hour.

title: Make the tools work for you
bullets: Privacy & data controls  —  find where to turn off model training on your data. Critical for NGOs handling sensitive beneficiary information.
Attachments, web search, tools  —  see what the input bar can actually do.
Free tools aren’t private by default

title: Defining AI problems and Solution Types
subtitle: The friction in your work is the starting point, not the tool.

title: Name your pain point
card: Fit a challenge into this template
My pain point at work is that I spend time (specific task).
If I could reduce or change this by (specific improvement),
I would gain (time, quality, or clarity impact).
question: Big challenges and small ones both count — a fundraising goal, or one type of email.

left.label: Weak
bullets: “My pain point is that I spend too much time trying to raise money. If I could fix this by getting more money, I’d gain greater impact.”
A real problem, but not one AI can directly solve. Specificity is missing.

right.label: Strong
bullets: “I spend hours answering repetitive enquiries about our programmes each week. If I automated even half, I’d gain ~5 hours a week for higher-value work.”
Specific task, specific change, measurable gain.

quote: Great! You’ve identified a problem. So how do we know whether AI can actually solve it?

rows.body: “I need to write or create something to share with others.”
“I want to learn something new or need better insight to make smarter choices.”
“I get a lot of questions and can’t keep up with answering them all.”
“I keep doing the same task over and over and it’s taking time away from more important work.”
“I spend too much time reviewing information that follows a pattern.”

left.label: Sounds like
left.body: “I need to write or create something to share with others.”

right.label: Examples
bullets: Drafting blog posts and newsletters
Building volunteer training materials
Generating a simple programme webpage

left.label: Sounds like

right.label: Examples
bullets: Handling repetitive enquiries from constituents
Answering staff questions about a new HR policy
A first-line FAQ for your programmes

title: Projects are great when you want to:
bullets: Create a specific AI “persona” that you work with over time.
Constrain Claude to just respond or react to a body of knowledge.
Have a Claude instance you want to share with teammates.

body: Writing strong grant/donor outreach applications and organizational content

title: Coach / Librarian
intro: “I want an AI that answers my and my team’s questions about our company materials.”
bullets: Prompt: Describe how it should interact with your team and your materials.
Files: Share the materials you want it to pull from.

title: The Organization Knowledge Base: Build a Claude Project for your organisation
card: Step 1. Start a new Claude Project and upload a document your team uses regularly.
(No document? Use your organisation's website copy, an old annual report, or your programme brochure.)
Step 2. Write a system prompt telling Claude to only answer questions using that document.
Example: "You are a helpful assistant for [org name]. Answer questions only using the materials provided. If the answer isn't in the document, say so."
Step 3. Try asking it: "What does our organisation do?" · "Who are our beneficiaries?" · "What did we achieve last year?"
question: The goal: Can a new volunteer or donor understand your work just by chatting with this assistant? Could your team use this instead of digging through folders?

title: Reflections

rows.lead: How well did the project follow your instructions?

kicker: The stronger your data is for the your projects, the better the outcomes will be. Invest time on clean data!

title: Lunch - See you at !
subtitle: Food, fresh air, and good conversation. You've earned it.

title: Where AI actually fits
bullets: Create & Communicate  —  “I need to write or create something to share with others.”
Learn & Decide  —  “I want to learn something new or need better insight to make smarter choices.”
Answer & Assist  —  “I get a lot of questions and can’t keep up with answering them all.”
Automate the Admin  —  “I keep doing the same task over and over and it’s taking time away from more important work.”
Sort & Scan  —  “I spend too much time reviewing information that follows a pattern.”

left.label: Sounds like
left.body: “I keep doing the same tasks over and over, and it’s taking time from more important work.”

right.label: Examples
bullets: Cross-checking volunteer timesheets across spreadsheets
Scanning receipts for expense reports
Reformatting data from one layout to another

title: Felt Pains
bullets: “I have to get all this data from Salesforce into Excel and then transform it.”
“I have to ensure all the data in my system is right / complete (e.g. all my grantees filled out every field in the grantee report portal).”
“I wish I could visualize / interact with some data in a more human way.”

title: Code(ish)!
bullets: Vanilla Claude Chat can write simple code for you!
Don’t think about it as “coding” - you are merely asking for a computer to do something annoying for you.
The mad lib to start with “Make me a (automation) that can run in my browser” “Make me an HTML file that…”
It’s very helpful to be very specific about the STEPS section of your prompt if your task is specific or complex.

title: Have Claude build your ideal data interface
card: Drag a data file you already use into the chat. (Beneficiary records, volunteer attendance, programme reach data, anything you track in a spreadsheet.)
Ask Claude to show you that data the way you actually want to see it:
How do you wish you could see this information?
Which numbers matter most to your team or funders?
How would you want to look at it (graphs, filters, sorting by district or programme)?
Example: "Can you look at our beneficiary data and make an interactive dashboard I can open in my browser, with a map by district and a breakdown by programme type?"
If you don't have data handy: Use your organisation's publicly available annual report numbers, or ask Claude to generate sample data that looks like yours so you can test the interface first.
question: → Ask Claude to show you that data the way you actually want to see it.

title: Claude Code — when chatting isn’t enough
subtitle: The same Claude you know, now working directly inside your files, folders and tools.

eyebrow: TWO WAYS TO WORK WITH CLAUDE
title: Claude Chat vs. Claude Code

left.label: Claude Chat
bullets: It talks  you type, it replies in the chat window.
You move the work  copy text in, copy the answer back out by hand.
Best for  thinking, drafting, summarising, answering questions.
Great for words — you still do the doing.

right.label: Claude Code
bullets: It acts  reads and edits your real files and runs the task itself.
It moves the work  opens folders, edits many documents at once.
Best for  repetitive, multi-file, “just change it” jobs.
Great for doing — it works where your files live.

eyebrow: TWO WAYS TO WORK WITH CLAUDE

title: How to use it — and where it fits
bullets: Point it at a folder — open Claude Code where the files you want to work with already live.
Ask in plain English — “rename these by date,” “pull the totals into one sheet,” “draft a report from these notes.”
Watch and approve — it shows each change before it makes it — nothing happens without your okay.
Use it where you work — on your desktop (Mac or Windows), in the editor, in the terminal, or on the web.
Connect your tools — link Google Drive, Gmail and more so it works with the data you already have.

bars.name: Use it in the browser (can’t access your computer)
Use it on your computer with low permissions (Don’t approve anything - just leave)
Use it on your computer with medium permissions (Review every approval)
Use it on your computer with lots of permissions (Approve most things)

left.label: Sounds like
left.body: “I spend too much time reviewing information that follows a pattern.”

right.label: Examples
bullets: Ranking volunteer or grant applications
Triaging incoming requests
Reviewing submissions against a rubric

title: Example: Help me sort these applications
card: ROLE AND GOAL: You are an expert technologist hiring manager. Your job is to help me assess the technologists in this spreadsheet.
STEPS:
Score each volunteer on their technology skills
Score each volunteer on how many roles they have around mentorship / advising
question: → Create a visualization of this list of scored candidates that I can run in my browser. Let me sort and filter them by all relevant fields.

title: Which type is yours?
intro: Run any problem against the five :-
bullets: Answer & Assist
Automate the Admin
Create & Communicate
Sort & Scan
Learn & Decide.

quote: The right tool follows the problem, never the other way round. Name the work first; the technology second.
